prompt_md: A class has a `double* m_data` member pointing to heap memory. The compiler-generated copy constructor is used. After `MyClass b{a}`, which statement is true?

options.a: `b.m_data` points to a new heap block with the same values as `a.m_data`.
options.b: `b.m_data` holds the same address as `a.m_data`, pointing to the same heap block.
options.c: `b.m_data` is null because the compiler cannot copy pointers.
options.d: The compiler refuses to compile the copy construction.

explanation_md: The compiler-generated copy constructor performs memberwise copy, which for a pointer member copies the address value — not the pointed-to data. Both `a.m_data` and `b.m_data` hold the same address, meaning both objects share one heap block.

The default copy constructor copies the pointer itself, so `b.m_ptr` and `a.m_ptr` point to the same `int`. Writing `99` through `b.m_ptr` changes the shared value, so reading through `a.m_ptr` also gives `99`.

prompt_md: What does the Rule of Three say about classes that manage heap resources?

options.a: Every class needs exactly three constructors.
options.b: If you provide a custom destructor, you almost certainly also need a custom copy constructor and a custom copy-assignment operator.
options.c: You must write three deep-copy functions for every class.
options.d: Classes with raw pointers must override all three comparison operators.

explanation_md: The Rule of Three says that the destructor, copy constructor, and copy-assignment operator form a group: if you need to write one (because the class owns a resource), you almost certainly need all three. Omitting the copy constructor or copy-assignment operator leaves the compiler-generated shallow versions in place, which are unsafe for classes that own heap memory.

options.a: The for loop should count down, not up.
options.b: The old `m_data` is not deleted before the new allocation, causing a memory leak.
options.c: Returning `*this` is incorrect — it should return `rhs`.
options.d: There is no bug; this is a correct deep copy.

explanation_md: Before allocating new storage, the operator must `delete[] m_data` to free the block that `m_data` currently points to. Without that line, every assignment leaves the old block unreachable on the heap — a memory leak. There is also no self-assignment guard, which would cause reading freed memory if `a = a` were called.

prompt_md: When is it safe to rely on the compiler-generated copy constructor (i.e., NOT write your own)?

options.a: When the class has any member that is a pointer.
options.b: When the class owns heap memory managed through a raw pointer.
options.c: When all members are value types or standard library containers that already handle their own copying.
options.d: Only when the class has exactly one data member.

explanation_md: Standard library types (`std::string`, `std::vector`, etc.) implement deep copy in their own copy constructors, so a class that uses only these (plus plain value types) is safe with the compiler-generated copy constructor — it just calls each member's own copy constructor in turn.
